item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone on reddit made a great point that I wanted to share: What if someone moves away from the town and the social bonds fade? Relocate the kid that got interviewed and ask him again in a couple of months…

That could work, but the Foundation doesn't know for sure. Socio anomalies are tricky. Besides, they've already got a rough outline of what happened here, and I imagine they wouldnt be able to sell the Ethics Committee on forcibly relocating a whole family just on a hunch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I saw the word "Indiana" I immediately came to the comments to see if it was written by djkaktus

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
my god, the dread i felt reading this article. the interview log ends perfectly.

(edit: clicked reply instead of new post. my bad.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of this, but as I mentioned in my previous comment, I think this could be fleshed out some more.

Here is another thing that got me confused:

SCP-2510 was discovered by Reba Sinclair, the aunt of a student at Oakhill, who traveled to Converse to attend their nephew's graduation.

seems to directly contradict

All individuals within Converse will refuse to acknowledge SCP-2510-1, ignoring it entirely when within the vicinity.

as auntie is in Converse and in the immediate vicinity of the body. I suppose this is meant to imply that she just isn't part of the local community enough to be affected. In that case, I suggest adjusting the second quote in order to reflect that more accurately.

Edit: Someone on reddit made a great point that I wanted to share: What if someone moves away from the town and the social bonds fade? Relocate the kid that got interviewed and ask him again in a couple of months…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do think this is a valid point, though. Let's say the Foundation draws the same conclusion as you (that the town might be the source of the anomaly), wouldn't they be interested in getting to the bottom of it and somehow containing it? By getting to the bottom of it, I mean that I understand one of the main objectices of the Foundation is to figure these things out. What exactly is the anomaly? Can it happen again? Can we maybe even use it for our own advantage somehow?

If it pertains to "protecting the high school football team", will it cause other incidents to ignored? Would it give some sort of "immunity" to players of the team? What if there are smarter players in the future, who will figure this effect out and use it for their advantage? I don't know, it just feels like something is missing here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember one being used as an advanced interrogator in an article which was recently deleted by its author, hopefully for rewrites. It wouldn't surprise me if it had precedent somewhere else.

Point is, no canon, you know the drill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I don't think I've seen Foundation telepaths in any article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well in this article, they don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation has telepaths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't like this, it feels like a Lifetime original movie was turned into an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was terrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Barnes: Anyone interesting attend it??"

Is this double-question mark intentional in the closing interview? Kind of broke the flow for me.

Otherwise, this is an article that left me wanting more, and not in the best of ways. Novote.
woot

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oof. How the hell did I miss that? Shall fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think in the interview log, the first time that Barnes speaks is listed as “Interviewer” but it’s Barnes for the rest of the log. Typo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting! I hadn't picked up on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amos: I mean, they're kinda dicks, If I'm being honest. But they're not that smart. They get through most things by dumb luck, from what I can tell. That's just what I think of them.

Barnes: …Ah. I'll keep that in mind.

What amos is trying to say here, and what Barnes is picking up here, is that he doesn't think Cosniak or Thompson were the source of the anomaly, but that they were just lucky.

If I had to point a finger towards anything, it'd be the town itself. Them going to college would actually make ot less likely for something like this to happen, whoch i think the Foundation understands.

The Foundation has a good understanding of what happened here. They don't know how it started, but they know what appeared to be the impetus. Due to the socio-antimeme, no one's gonna talk to them, and its self-containing. So they've administered a few amnestics, and took away the body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this and upvoted right after reading it! But after thinking about it for a little while, I'm afraid it might fall into the "readers are smarter than the Foundation" trap. It isn't hard for a reader to infer what happened here, and it seems like Agent Barnes has at least an inkling, too. But it seems like a criminally negligent lack of action in the Foundation's response!

The anomaly MIGHT be unique to Samantha McArthur. But what if the source of it is Kevin Cosniak? Or Derek Thompson? Why not have an undercover agent embedded to keep an eye on them?

Otherwise, there might be a string of unremembered corpses in bathrooms following one or both of them through college.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my thoughts exactly. it's 3935, but more to the point, in a good way. and it does end up taking a different path to get to the same destination. i liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost feels like an homage to 3935 but simple, direct. I like it. The small town with a dark secret is a common trope but can be used in so many interesting ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember this from last year… Maybe I critted or something.

Anyways, this is relatively short and quite dreadful. In a fantastic way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So glad you took my advice! Definitely improved, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you've decided to take the "we can't talk about it" route. The piece was effective enough even considering I knew what the twist was, and the interview was structured rather nicely.

This is, I think, the 5th version of this skip I've seen, and I think it's the best one. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very few things manage to incite dread properly. You need to be cognizant of the possible ending, and there can't be any subversions along the way. As a consequence, the plot often feels too obvious when not done correctly.

I am, pleased to say, however, that this piece handles dread wonderfully. We are made aware of what is to come, and yet, we remain curious enough to keep reading. That alone, in my opinion, deserves an upvote - and the article fails convince me to vote otherwise.

Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why when we do our darkest deeds
Do we tell?
They burn in our brains
Become a living hell
'Cause everybody tells
Everybody tells

Well, here it is. This is something I've been working on for almost six months now. Gone through a torrent of revisions, draft threads, rewrites, and finally ended up on the mainlist.

Thank you to Tanhony, Yossipossi, DrAkimoto, Oboebandgeek, and stormbreath for reviewing this. Thank you to everyone else for all the help you gave on previous versions of this, wouldn't be here without you guys.

While you're here, be sure to check out some of my other works.

Disclaimer: The old 2510 was deleted due to plagiarism, which I had nothing to do with. This is an entirely separate article that I placed into the available slot.

Now with audio adaptation by TheeSherm!
